source: CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
title: Ensure auditing is configured in the IBM Cloud account

input_row.source: CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
input_row.title: Ensure auditing is configured in the IBM Cloud account
input_row.description: Collect audit events from IBM Cloud resources so that you can monitor activity in your IBM Cloud account.
input_row.rationale: IBM Cloud™ Activity Tracker with LogDNA is a Cloud service that you can use to monitor the activity of your IBM Cloud account. For example, you can use this service to investigate abnormal activity and critical actions, and comply with regulatory audit requirements. The audit events that are collected comply with the Cloud Auditing Data Federation (CADF) standard. Each audit event includes information about the initiator of the action, the action that is run, the target resource on which the action is requested, the observer, and the outcome of the action. For more information about the format, see Event fields. Activity Tracker collects two types of events for audit-enabled services in the IBM Cloud: • Management events • Data events Data events access or modify customer data. Management events report on operational actions on IBM Cloud resources. Both types of events generate events for create, read, update, and delete (CRUD) actions. Every user that accesses the IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service in your account must be assigned an access policy with an IAM user role defined. The policy determines what actions the user can perform within the context of the service or instance that you select. The allowable actions are customized and defined as operations that are allowed to be performed on the service. The actions are then mapped to IAM user roles. Tip: To organize a set of users and service IDs into a single entity that makes it easy for you to manage IAM permissions, use access groups. You can assign a single policy to the group instead of assigning the same access multiple times for each individual user or service ID. The following types of roles are available in IBM Cloud: • Platform management roles enable users to perform tasks on service resources at the platform level, for example, assign user access for the service, create or delete service IDs, create instances, assign policies for your service to other users, and bind instances to applications. • Service access roles enable users to be assigned varying levels of permission for calling the service's API, such as viewing data and configuring alerts. Impact: Failure to meet this control limits your ability to monitor activity in your IBM Cloud account. It also restricts your ability to identify the initiator of actions in your account, the resources affected, and details about the request. In addition, if IAM permissions are not set properly, you can expose your organization’s audit information to non-authorized users.
input_row.audit: To check that you are collecting audit events from IBM Cloud resources so that you can monitor activity in your IBM Cloud account, you can use the following check list to ensure that auditing is configured in an IBM Cloud account: 1. Ensure that service instances of the IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service are provisioned in each region where you operate in the Cloud. Only one Activity Tracker instance is allowed in each IBM Cloud location where the service is available. 2. Ensure that an Activity Tracker instance is provisioned in Frankfurt. This instance acts as a global domain and collects audit events that report user actions in the IBM Cloud where the scope expands beyond a single region, such as managing users. 3. Ensure that access control groups (ACG) are configured in your account to manage user access. a. Check that you have multiple access groups to manage permissions to operate audit service instances in IBM Cloud, and to view and manage audit data. b. Check each access group documents the scope of the type of access users get when they are added to the group. c. Check the policies and roles that are configured in each access group. They must restrict access to users or service IDs taking into account the scope of the access group. d. Check that the users that are added to each access group are correct taking into account their role in the Cloud account. To check if audit instances are provisioned and configured in the account, complete the following steps: Note: To complete these steps, your IBM Cloud user ID (IBMid) must have the following roles: • A platform role with viewer role for the IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service for the account • A service role with reader role for the IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service for the account • Identity and Access Management (IAM) permissions to view access groups in the account UI instructions: 1. Log in to IBM Cloud at https://cloud.ibm.com. 2. Go to the Menu icon. Then, select Observability to access the Observability dashboard. 3. Select Activity Tracker from the page navigation menu 4. Check that you can see an Activity Tracker instance in Frankfurt and one instance for each location where you operate in the IBM Cloud. 5. To launch the LogDNA web UI, for each instance, select View LogDNA. 6. In the LogDNA web UI, verify that you can see audit events, and that they comply with the IBM event format that is documented. For more information about the format, see Event fields. CLI instructions: 1. Log in to IBM Cloud. Run the following command: ibmcloud login -a cloud.ibm.com For a federated account, run the following command: ibmcloud login -a cloud.ibm.com --sso 2. List all the audit instances that are provisioned in your account. Run the following command: ibmcloud resource service-instances --service-name logdnaat --all- resource-groups 3. Check that you have one instance in Frankfurt, and one instance for each location where you operate in the IBM Cloud. Note: You can view events through the LogDNA web UI only. Therefore, you must launch the LogDNA web UI from the IBM Cloud console to verify that events are being collected. To verify that access groups are available in the account to manage permissions working with audit service instances, complete the following steps: UI instructions: 1. Log in to IBM Cloud at https://cloud.ibm.com. 2. From the Menu bar, select Manage > Access (IAM) . 3. Select Access groups from the page navigation menu. 4. Find the access groups that grant permissions to work with the IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service. 5. Check the description to identify the scope. 6. Check the list of users and service IDs. Verify that they should be members of the group. 7. Check that policies and roles are aligned with the scope of the access group. CLI instructions: 1. Log in to IBM Cloud. Run the following command: ibmcloud login -a cloud.ibm.com For a federated account, run the following command: ibmcloud login -a cloud.ibm.com --sso 2. List the access groups in the account. Run the following command: ibmcloud iam access-groups Repeat the following steps for each access group that you identify. 3. Get information about each access group. Run the following command to list the details of an access group: ibmcloud iam access-group {Access Group Name} --output JSON Check the description to identify the scope. 4. Check the list of users and service IDs. Verify that they should be members of the group. To get the list of users that belong to an access group, run the following command: ibmcloud iam access-group-users {Access Group Name}--output JSON To get the list of service IDs that belong to an access group, run the following command: ibmcloud iam access-group-service-ids {Access Group Name} --output JSON 5. Check that policies and roles are aligned with the scope of the access group. Run the following command to get details of the policies and roles configured for the access group: ibmcloud iam access-group-policies {Access Group Name} --output JSON
input_row.remediation: 1. If an Activity Tracker instance is not provisioned in each of the locations where you operate in IBM Cloud, you must provision an instance of the service. To provision an audit instance, choose any of the following options: o Provision an instance through the IBM Cloud Catalog. o Provision an instance through the Observability dashboard. o Provision an instance through the CLI. 2. If an Activity Tracker instance is not provisioned in Frankfurt, you must provision one. To provision an audit instance, choose any of the following options: o Provision an instance through the IBM Cloud Catalog. o Provision an instance through the Observability dashboard. o Provision an instance through the CLI. 3. If you are missing access groups or policies, complete the following steps to remediate the problem: o If access groups are not configured, you must create one or more access groups. When you create, or when you edit an access group, you can add a description to the access group. o If the access group to manage administration permissions is missing policies, assign administrative policies to the access group. o If the access group to manage user permissions is missing policies, assign user policies to the access group. o If you want to add users to the access group, see Add users to the access group. o If you want to add service IDs to the access group, see Add service IDs to the access group. o If you need to remove users or service IDs, see Removing access. o If you need to modify a policy, see Editing existing access. Default Value: By default, management audit events are generated and collected automatically. However, you must configure the IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service in your account to view and manage events, and to be notified of specific scenarios. For most of the services in IBM Cloud, data events are generated and collected automatically. However, there are some exceptions where you must opt-in to collect them, such as IBM Cloud Object Storage. References: 1. IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA: Adoption guidelines for regulated and highly available workloads: https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/Activity-Tracker-with- LogDNA?topic=logdnaat-adoption 2. IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service plans: https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/Activity-Tracker-with-LogDNA?topic=logdnaat- service_plan 3. Managing access for IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA: https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/Activity-Tracker-with-LogDNA?topic=logdnaat-iam
input_row.impact: Failure to meet this control limits your ability to monitor activity in your IBM Cloud account. It also restricts your ability to identify the initiator of actions in your account, the resources affected, and details about the request. In addition, if IAM permissions are not set properly, you can expose your organization’s audit information to non-authorized users.
input_row.references: 1. IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA: Adoption guidelines for regulated and highly available workloads: https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/Activity-Tracker-with- LogDNA?topic=logdnaat-adoption 2. IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service plans: https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/Activity-Tracker-with-LogDNA?topic=logdnaat- service_plan 3. Managing access for IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA: https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/Activity-Tracker-with-LogDNA?topic=logdnaat-iam

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
This compliance control can be fully verified using IBM Cloud APIs and IBM Cloud CLI. The setup involves checking for the presence and configuration of the IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service instances across multiple regions, which are standard IBM Cloud service properties and can be queried through the IBM Cloud CLI commands and APIs.

**PROGRAM NAME:** ibm_activity_tracker_instance_configuration_verified

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
The automated program will check for the presence and configuration of IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA instances as follows:

1. **IBM Cloud CLI Commands:**
   - Use `ibmcloud resource service-instances --service-name logdnaat --all-resource-groups` to list all Activity Tracker service instances.
   - Confirm that there is an Activity Tracker instance provisioned in Frankfurt and one in each location where operations are conducted.

2. **Access Control Verification via IBM Cloud CLI:**
   - Use `ibmcloud iam access-groups` to list all access groups.
   - For each access group, use `ibmcloud iam access-group {Access Group Name} --output JSON` to retrieve details about scope and configuration.
   - Validate access control setups by using:
     - `ibmcloud iam access-group-users {Access Group Name} --output JSON` to list users.
     - `ibmcloud iam access-group-service-ids {Access Group Name} --output JSON` to list service IDs.
     - `ibmcloud iam access-group-policies {Access Group Name} --output JSON` to examine policies and roles.

3. **Configuration Check:**
   - Verify that the audit events conform to the IBM event format as documented.

4. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - **Pass**: An Activity Tracker instance is found in Frankfurt and each operational location, service roles and policies are applied correctly in access groups, and events are being collected in the correct format.
   - **Fail**: Missing instances in described locations, incorrect access group configurations, or failure to conform to event format.

Using these methods, the automated program will comprehensively verify that the IBM Cloud account is configured for auditing as per CIS requirements.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:** The classification as "Automated" is mostly correct, as the majority of the checks can be performed using IBM Cloud APIs and IBM Cloud CLI commands. However, the verification of event formats might require manual inspection or additional scripting to ensure compliance with specific format requirements, which could involve some manual oversight.
- **IBM Cloud APIs/CLI Capabilities:** The use of IBM Cloud CLI commands for listing service instances and verifying access controls is appropriate. However, the assessment could be enhanced by incorporating IBM Cloud SDKs or IBM Cloud Config for more comprehensive checks and potential integration into CI/CD pipelines.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 7

- **Automation Details:** The automation details are generally correct, but there are areas for improvement. For example, the command `ibmcloud resource service-instances --service-name logdnaat --all-resource-groups` is valid for listing service instances, but ensuring the correct configuration might require additional checks or API calls to verify detailed settings.
- **IBM Cloud CLI Methods:** The CLI commands for access group verification are valid, but the process could be streamlined by using scripts to automate the parsing and validation of JSON outputs.
- **Program Name:** The program name `ibm_activity_tracker_instance_configuration_verified` is somewhat descriptive but could be more specific. It should indicate the specific checks being performed, such as location verification or format compliance.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Missing Considerations:** The assessment does not explicitly mention the verification of audit log retention periods or encryption settings, which are critical for compliance. These should be included in the automation details.
- **Alternative Methods:** Consider using IBM Cloud Config to define and enforce compliance policies, which can provide a more robust and scalable solution.
- **Program Name Suggestion:** A more descriptive name could be `ibm_activity_tracker_audit_setup_verification`, which better reflects the scope of the checks being performed.

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- While the assessment covers the main points, the lack of detail on certain aspects such as event format verification and retention policies reduces confidence. Additionally, the reliance solely on CLI commands without exploring IBM Cloud Config or SDKs limits the robustness of the approach.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- **Modify:** Enhance the automation details to include checks for log retention periods and encryption settings. Consider integrating IBM Cloud Config for policy enforcement and compliance tracking. Update the program name to better reflect the specific checks being performed.
- **Re-classify:** Consider a hybrid approach, acknowledging that while most checks can be automated, some manual verification might be necessary for event format compliance.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
The presence and configuration of IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service instances can be fully verified using IBM Cloud CLI and API calls. All required properties—including service instance existence per region and specific location (e.g., Frankfurt)—are exposed via IBM Cloud resource APIs. While event format validation could be more granular, the control’s audit steps and remediation focus on the provisioning and regional presence of Activity Tracker instances, which are directly automatable. Both AI opinions agree automation is feasible, with only minor concerns about deep event format validation, which is not explicitly required by the control’s audit steps.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
ibm_activity_tracker_service_instance_regional_presence

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- IBM Cloud Resource/Service: Activity Tracker with LogDNA service instances
- API/CLI Method:  
  - IBM Cloud CLI: `ibmcloud resource service-instances --service-name logdnaat --all-resource-groups`
  - IBM Cloud API: `GET /v2/resource_instances?name=logdnaat`
- Properties to Check:
  - Each required IBM Cloud region (including Frankfurt) has exactly one Activity Tracker instance provisioned.
  - The service instance status is "active".
- Pass Criteria:
  - For every region where the account operates (including Frankfurt), there is one and only one active Activity Tracker instance.
- Fail Criteria:
  - Any required region is missing an Activity Tracker instance, or there is more than one per region, or any instance is not active.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
The second opinion raised a minor concern about event format validation, but the control’s audit steps and remediation focus solely on service instance presence and regional coverage, which are fully automatable. Therefore, automation is appropriate and sufficient for this control.

**SOURCE:** CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
